title: AP U.S. History DBQ Practice Exam 07

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which the Great Migration transformed African American life and northern urban society from 1910 to 1940.
Document 1
Author/Origin: Described migration map created for this practice exam, United States, 1910–1940.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows arrows moving from Mississippi, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana, South Carolina, and Texas toward Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, New York, Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, and St. Louis. The largest arrows lead from the rural South to Chicago and Detroit. A note explains that migration increased during World War I, continued through the 1920s, and slowed during the Great Depression but did not stop.
Sourcing Cues: The map emphasizes internal migration from the South to industrial and urban centers in the North and Midwest.
Document 2
Author/Origin: Original simulated letter from a Black migrant to relatives in Mississippi, Chicago, 1917; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Primary text source.
“The packinghouse work is cold and hard, but the pay is more than I ever saw for a week’s labor in the Delta. I do not say Chicago is free of insult or danger. Landlords crowd us into certain blocks, and some white workers resent our arrival. Still, a man may vote here, read the paper without fear, and hope his children will see a different road.”
Sourcing Cues: The letter reflects the perspective of a Black southern migrant weighing economic opportunity, racial discrimination, political rights, and urban challenges.
Document 3
Author/Origin: Original simulated editorial from a southern county newspaper, Georgia, 1918; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Newspaper-style excerpt.
“Labor agents have filled the countryside with promises of northern wages. Farms now struggle to find hands at planting time, and families who once knew their place are being encouraged to abandon the communities that fed them. Local leaders must act before outside agitators empty the fields.”
Sourcing Cues: The editorial reflects the viewpoint of white southern elites concerned about labor control, racial hierarchy, and the loss of Black agricultural workers.
Document 4
Author/Origin: Industrial labor table created for this practice exam, selected northern cities, 1910–1930.
Source Type: Data table.
The table is titled “Selected Changes in Black Urban Employment.”
City | Common job growth for Black workers, 1910–1930 | Common limits
Chicago | meatpacking, steel, railroads, domestic service | segregated neighborhoods, job discrimination
Detroit | automobile factories, foundries, service work | racial tension, unequal promotion
Pittsburgh | steel, railroads, hotels, domestic service | dangerous work, exclusion from many skilled jobs
New York | service work, garment-related labor, port work | high rents, housing discrimination
A note beneath the table states: “Industrial labor demand expanded opportunities but did not eliminate discrimination in hiring, housing, or unions.”
Sourcing Cues: The table highlights both economic opportunity and continuing racial barriers.
Document 5
Author/Origin: Original simulated report from a northern urban reform organization, Detroit, 1925; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Reform report excerpt.
“New arrivals require housing assistance, employment information, legal advice, and protection from fraudulent landlords. Churches, mutual-aid groups, women’s clubs, and neighborhood associations have become essential institutions. The city cannot pretend that migration is temporary; it must plan for schools, health services, and fair housing.”
Sourcing Cues: The report reflects urban reformers’ and community organizations’ concern with settlement, housing, and institution-building among migrants.
Document 6
Author/Origin: Described photograph from a Black neighborhood business district, Chicago, circa 1928.
Source Type: Described photograph.
The photograph shows a busy street with a newspaper office, a beauty salon, a church entrance, a small grocery, and a political club office. Men and women in coats stand near storefront windows advertising concerts, lectures, and fraternal society meetings. A streetcar line runs through the background.
Sourcing Cues: The photograph illustrates the growth of Black urban institutions, businesses, political organizations, and cultural life.
Document 7
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement from an interracial labor committee, Cleveland, 1935; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Labor organization statement.
“Employers have long used race to divide workers. Migrants from the South and workers born in the North must insist on equal hiring, equal pay, and union membership without color lines. A strike cannot win if one group is told another group is the enemy.”
Sourcing Cues: The statement reflects labor organizing during the New Deal era and the challenge of building interracial working-class solidarity.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point for a historically defensible claim evaluating the extent to which the Great Migration transformed African American life and northern urban society from 1910 to 1940. The thesis must make an argument, such as that the Great Migration significantly changed Black political, economic, cultural, and community life while also creating new forms of segregation, labor conflict, and racial tension in northern cities.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing broader relevant context, such as Jim Crow segregation, racial violence in the South, agricultural hardship, boll weevil damage, World War I labor demand, industrialization in northern cities, racial discrimination in housing and labor, or the Great Depression.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point for accurately using content from at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points for using content from at least six documents to support a coherent argument about migration routes, economic opportunity, southern labor control, urban employment, housing and community organizations, Black business and culture, or interracial labor organizing.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point for using at least one specific, relevant piece of outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the Chicago Defender, Red Summer of 1919, Harlem Renaissance, NAACP growth, National Urban League, Marcus Garvey and the UNIA, restrictive covenants, race riots in East St. Louis or Chicago, World War I industrial labor demand, or New Deal labor organizing.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. For example, a student might analyze the migrant letter’s mixed view of opportunity and discrimination, the southern editorial’s concern with labor control, the reform report’s purpose of pushing municipal planning, the photograph’s evidence of community-building, or the labor committee’s effort to overcome employer divide-and-rule strategies.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a nuanced argument, such as explaining that the Great Migration expanded freedom and opportunity while exposing migrants to new forms of segregation; comparing southern and northern racial systems; distinguishing economic, political, and cultural effects; or showing how migrants transformed cities even as cities shaped migrants’ experiences.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ would likely argue that the Great Migration transformed African American life to a large extent by expanding wage labor opportunities, voting possibilities, community institutions, Black businesses, and cultural life in northern cities. It should also explain limits and conflicts, including housing segregation, job discrimination, racial violence, southern resistance to labor loss, and tensions within industrial workplaces. Useful document groupings include migration routes, migrant opportunity, southern white resistance, northern labor markets, community-building, urban culture, and interracial labor organizing. Relevant outside evidence could include the Chicago Defender, Red Summer, NAACP, National Urban League, Harlem Renaissance, restrictive covenants, or World War I industrial labor demand.
